title: Listening - Part 3: Secrets Beneath the Streets
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.
instructions: You will hear an interview with two archaeologists, Dr. Julian Ross and Sarah Miller, about the challenges of excavating in modern cities. For questions 15 – 20, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which fits best according to what you hear.

extracts.context: An interview discussing the pressure of time and the preservation of history in developing urban areas.
extracts.audio_script:
Interviewer: Exploring ancient ruins is one thing, but digging under a busy London street is quite another. Julian, what’s the biggest hurdle for an urban archaeologist?

Julian: It’s undoubtedly the clock. [15]We are usually only given a tiny window of opportunity between a building being demolished and the foundations for a new skyscraper being poured.[/15] It’s a high-pressure race against the developers' schedule. You’re trying to carefully brush away dirt while a crane is literally looming over your shoulder.

Interviewer: Sarah, you recently worked on the 'Crossrail' project. Did you find anything that changed the history books?

Sarah: We found a massive plague pit, which was harrowing but incredibly informative. But [16]what really fascinated me were the everyday items—discarded leather shoes and broken pottery—that told us more about the 14th-century middle class than any royal record ever could.[/16] It’s the history of the ordinary person that cities preserve so well.

Interviewer: Julian, some people argue that we shouldn't let archaeology stop progress. If we found a Roman villa under a hospital site, what should we do?

Julian: It’s a delicate balance. [17]I’m actually a proponent of 'preservation by record'—we dig it, we map it, we scan it in 3D, and then we let the hospital be built.[/17] We can't turn every city into a museum. What matters is that the data is saved for future generations, even if the physical stones are covered up again.

Interviewer: Sarah, technology must be changing how you work.

Sarah: Ground-penetrating radar is a lifesaver. [18]It allows us to see walls and voids beneath the pavement without ever lifting a single stone.[/18] It means we can be much more surgical in our excavations. We don't have to dig a hundred holes; we dig the one hole where we know the treasure—or the trash—actually is.

Interviewer: Is there a sense of competition between archaeologists in different cities?

Julian: [19]It’s more of a shared frustration with the lack of international standards for urban digging.[/19] Some cities have great laws to protect heritage, while in others, history is simply bulldozed in the name of profit. We’re constantly sharing notes on how to navigate the legal minefield of different municipalities.

Interviewer: Finally, what’s the most surprising thing you’ve found?

Sarah: [20]For me, it was a perfectly preserved 18th-century wig in a London sewer.[/20] It was a bizarre reminder of the vanity of the past. Julian once found a medieval cat’s paw print in a floor tile, didn't you? It’s those tiny, human moments that make the mud and the rain worth it.

questions.question: Julian says that the main difficulty for archaeologists in cities is
options: A) the danger of working near large cranes.
B) the lack of space to perform excavations.
C) the limited time available before construction starts.
D) the difficulty of demolishing old buildings.
questions.explanation: Julian explains they only have a 'tiny window of opportunity' between demolition and new construction.

questions.question: What did Sarah find particularly interesting about the Crossrail project?
options: A) the discovery of ancient royal records
B) the information provided by common household objects
C) the harrowing experience of digging a plague pit
D) the history of the upper class in the 14th century
questions.explanation: She was fascinated by everyday items like shoes and pottery that revealed details about ordinary people.

questions.question: Regarding the conflict between history and modern needs, Julian believes that
options: A) all Roman villas should be turned into museums.
B) hospital construction should always be delayed for archaeology.
C) digital documentation can be a substitute for physical preservation.
D) physical stones are more important than 3D scans.
questions.explanation: He supports 'preservation by record'—mapping and scanning findings so that building work can proceed.

questions.question: How does ground-penetrating radar benefit Sarah’s work?
options: A) It eliminates the need to dig any holes at all.
B) It identifies exactly where to excavate to find items.
C) It turns pavement into a more flexible material.
D) It allows her to lift heavy stones more easily.
questions.explanation: She says it allows them to be 'surgical' and dig only where they know something is located.

questions.question: Julian notes that archaeologists from different cities often discuss
options: A) their competitive feelings toward one another.
B) the best ways to bulldoze historical sites for profit.
C) the inconsistencies in legal protections for heritage.
D) the lack of treasure found in modern municipalities.
questions.explanation: He mentions a shared frustration with the lack of international standards and sharing notes on legal minefields.

questions.question: What does Sarah describe as her most unusual discovery?
options: A) a medieval floor tile with a cat's footprint
B) a bizarre reminder of vanity found in a sewer
C) a collection of perfectly preserved royal records
D) the rain and mud she faces during every dig
questions.explanation: She mentions an 18th-century wig found in a sewer as a bizarre reminder of past vanity.
